glossary.def: Handshape: A | Orientation: palm in | Location: neutral space | Movement: double bounce hand down | Note: This sign is similar to SUITCASE, which is made with an S handshape.

glossary.def: Orientation: palm left | Location: in front of face | Movement: circle thumb around face | Note: This sign is disparaging to African Americans. See page 139 for the preferred sign.

Movement: slide right middle finger across back of left hand
Nonmanual signal: right shoulder raised
flashcards.source: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.

flashcards.front:
What sign is shown?
Handshape: Open 8 : S | Location: neutral space
flashcards.back:
EARTH

Handshape: Open 8 : S
Orientation: p alms down, hands crossed, right middle finger and thumb grip left hand
Location: neutral space
Movement: rock right hand side to side bare, bald, blank,
flashcards.source: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.

flashcards.front:
What sign is shown?
Handshape: Open 8 : S | Location: neutral space
flashcards.back:
EMPTY, NAKED, NUDE, VACANT, VOID

Handshape: Open 8 : S
Orientation: p alms down, knuckles forward, right finger above left hand
Location: neutral space
Movement: slide right middle finger down back
Nonmanual signal: raise right shoulder
flashcards.source: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.

flashcards.front:
What sign is shown?
Handshape: Open 8 > 8 : Open 8 > 8 | Location: middle fingers and thumbs on chest
flashcards.back:
INTERESTING

Handshape: Open 8 > 8 : Open 8 > 8
Orientation: palms in, right hand above left hand
Location: middle fingers and thumbs on chest
Movement: move hands forward with an exaggerated motion, closing thumbs and middle fingers
Nonmanual signal: body leans forward; eyes widen; mouth open
flashcards.source: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.

flashcards.front:
What sign is shown?
Handshape: Open > S Open B 8 : | Location: right middle finger on left side of chest; left hand in neutral space
flashcards.back:
HEART ATTACK

Handshape: Open > S Open B 8 :
Orientation: palms in
Location: right middle finger on left side of chest; left hand in neutral space
Movement: move right hand out, closing hand, smack hand into left palm
Nonmanual signal: body rigid when fist hits grease, fat, gravy,
flashcards.source: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.

flashcards.front:
What sign is shown?
Handshape: Open 8 > 8 : Open B | Location: neutral space
flashcards.back:
GREASY, OIL

Handshape: Open 8 > 8 : Open B
Orientation: right palm up, thumb and middle finger gripping edge of left hand; left palm right
Location: neutral space
Movement: brush right thumb and middle finger down and off edge of left hand twice
Nonmanual signal: “disgusted” expression
flashcards.source: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.

flashcards.front:
What sign is shown?
Handshape: S Open 8 > 5 : | Location: neutral space
flashcards.back:
SODA POP

Handshape: S Open 8 > 5 :
Orientation: right palm down, middle finger inside index finger; left palm right
Location: neutral space
Movement: lift right hand up, opening hand, then smack down on top of left hand
flashcards.source: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.

flashcards.front:
What sign is shown?
Handshape: Open 8 > 5 : 5 > Open 8 | Location: neutral space
flashcards.back:
JESUS

Handshape: Open 8 > 5 : 5 > Open 8
Orientation: palms facing
Location: neutral space
Movement: tap right middle finger on left palm, then tap left middle finger on right palm Bible (Christian)
flashcards.source: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.

flashcards.front:
What sign is shown?
Handshape: Open 8 > 5 > Open B : Open 8 > 5 > Open B | Location: neutral space
flashcards.back:
+ (COMPOUND: JESUS BOOK)

Handshape: Open 8 > 5 > Open B : Open 8 > 5 > Open B
Orientation: palms facing
Location: neutral space
Movement: tap right middle finger on left palm, then tap left middle finger on right palm; change to Open B handshape, palms facing and touching, and open hands out
flashcards.source: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.

notes.title: How to Use the ASL Handshape Dictionary
notes.body:
SOURCE: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.
COURSE: SL1 — American Sign Language

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ABOUT THIS DICTIONARY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The ASL Handshape Dictionary organizes signs by the HANDSHAPE used to produce them — not by English word order. This makes it ideal for:
• Looking up a sign you have SEEN but don't know the meaning of
• Learning all signs that share a common handshape
• Understanding the PARAMETERS of ASL signs

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THE FIVE PARAMETERS OF ASL SIGNS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Every ASL sign is defined by five parameters. Changing any one parameter changes the meaning:

1. HANDSHAPE — the configuration of the hand(s)
   Examples: A, B, C, Open A, Bent 5, etc.

2. ORIENTATION — which direction the palm faces
   Examples: palm in, palm out, palm left, palm down

3. LOCATION — where on or near the body the sign is made
   Examples: neutral space, forehead, chin, chest, cheek

4. MOVEMENT — how the hand(s) move
   Examples: double bounce down, arc left to right, circle on chest, tap twice

5. NONMANUAL SIGNALS (NMS) — facial expressions, mouth movements, body posture
   Examples: head nod, raised eyebrows, shoulder shrug, "sad" expression
   NMS are GRAMMATICALLY essential in ASL — they are not optional.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
HOW SIGNS ARE ORGANIZED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Section 1: ONE-HAND SIGNS (pages 40–150) — organized by handshape A through 5
• Section 2: TWO-HAND SIGNS (pages 151–370) — organized by handshape of dominant hand
• INDEX (pages 371–419) — alphabetical English glosses with page references

TWO-HAND SIGN NOTATION:
  H1 : H2 — both hands use the same static handshape
  H1 > H2 — dominant hand changes from one handshape to another
  Passive hand is listed after the colon (:)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
READING AN ENTRY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Each entry includes:
  GLOSS — the English word(s) representing the sign (in bold)
  ILLUSTRATION — a line drawing of the signer performing the sign
  Handshape, Orientation, Location, Movement
  Nonmanual signal (when required)
  Note (variant signs, cultural notes, preferred alternatives)

★ IMPORTANT: One English gloss does NOT equal one ASL sign.
  ASL is a complete, independent language with its own grammar.
  Glosses are TRANSLATIONS, not direct equivalents.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY FACTS ABOUT ASL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ ASL is a complete, natural language with its own grammar — NOT signed English
★ ASL is the primary language of the American Deaf community
★ Each country has its own sign language (BSL, LSF, JSL, etc.)
★ Nonmanual signals carry grammatical meaning (questions, negation, topic marking)
★ Dominant hand = the signer's preferred hand (usually right)
★ Neutral space = the area in front of the signer's body
★ Signs are culturally situated — some signs have been updated or replaced
notes.source: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.

notes.title: ASL Handshape Reference Guide
notes.body:
SOURCE: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
COMMON HANDSHAPE DESCRIPTIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
A         — Fist with thumb resting on side of index finger
Open A    — Fist with thumb extended upward (hitchhiker thumb)
B         — Flat hand, fingers together and extended, thumb folded across palm
Open B    — Flat hand, fingers together and extended, thumb also extended
Bent B    — B handshape with fingers bent at knuckles (90°)
C         — Hand curved into a C shape
Baby O    — O handshape but smaller; fingertips touch thumb in a small circle
D         — Index finger extended and curved to touch thumb; other fingers extended
E         — All fingers bent, fingertips touch thumb
F         — Index finger and thumb touch to form a circle; other fingers extended
G         — Index finger and thumb extended horizontally, parallel to ground
H         — Index and middle fingers extended together, horizontal
I         — Pinky finger extended, others folded; thumb may rest on fingers
K         — Index and middle fingers extended in V shape, thumb between them
L         — Index finger extended upward, thumb extended horizontally (like letter L)
O         — All fingertips touch thumb to form an O
Open 8    — Middle finger extended while others are open/spread
R         — Index and middle fingers crossed
S         — Fist with thumb folded over fingers
T         — Fist with thumb tucked between index and middle fingers
V         — Index and middle fingers extended in a V (peace sign)
Bent V    — V handshape with both fingers bent at middle knuckle
W         — Ring, middle, and index fingers extended and spread
X         — Index finger hooked/bent at knuckle (crooked index)
Y         — Pinky and thumb extended, others folded
1         — Index finger pointing up (same as D without curved finger)
5         — All five fingers spread open
Bent 5    — 5 handshape with all fingers bent at knuckles
Flattened O — O handshape opened and flattened

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
TWO-HAND SIGN TYPES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SYMMETRICAL — both hands mirror each other
ASYMMETRICAL — dominant hand moves; passive hand is stationary base
ALTERNATING — hands take turns moving
H1 : H2 notation — dominant : passive handshape
notes.source: Tennant, R.A. & Brown, M.G. (1998). The American Sign Language Handshape Dictionary. Gallaudet University Press.
